ranks.C: Chrom comes across Olivia practicing her dance alone in a quiet corner of camp, and her painful shyness takes over — she stammers, nearly flees, and the two lapse into long awkward silences. He's struck by the little he glimpses and careful not to crowd her, but barely a sentence passes between them.
ranks.B: Chrom psyches himself up to win Olivia over with easy charm, then immediately fumbles it with a teasing joke about throwing a poor dancer out of the Shepherds, which only sends her into a panic. He scrambles to explain he was kidding, and the clumsy, well-meant effort begins to thaw the distance between them.
ranks.A: Chrom asks Olivia to dance just for him and learns she freezes off the battlefield even though she never hesitates in combat. Talk turns to Basilio, who once rescued her from a predatory noble in her theater days, and when she laughs without nerves for the first time, Chrom tells her — to her embarrassment — how lovely it was.
ranks.S: Chrom confesses that what he feels is real and more than a little mad: he barely knows her, yet can't stop thinking of her, and he resolves to follow his heart as his sister always did. He proposes and presses the royal ring of House Ylisse into her hands; stunned, Olivia says yes.

ranks.C: Worn down by the endless campaign, Chrom stumbles and nearly falls in front of Sumia — blaming a stray pebble — and admits how exhausting it is to always project strength as the army's commander. He swears her to secrecy, and she's quietly moved that he trusted her with it at all.
ranks.B: Delighted that she now shares Chrom's first real secret, Sumia gently strong-arms him into actually resting: she'll keep his confidence, but only if he naps before the strategy meeting, or she'll tell everyone a mere pebble bested the mighty Chrom. He gives in, bemused at how easily she gets her way.
ranks.A: Sumia bakes Chrom a rhubarb pie to restore him, brushing off his hope for something meatier with cheerful insistence that vegetables are what a tired body needs. He braces to choke it down out of politeness — and is genuinely amazed to find it delicious, the domestic warmth between them now unmistakable.
ranks.S: Chrom realizes the steadiness Sumia brings him has become something he can't do without, and proposes. He gives her the royal ring crafted to mark his birth and handed down by his father, and the two seal it — with a running joke that their days ahead will be full of pies.

ranks.C: Chrom finds Sully tending the fire and flatly refusing the cooking and sewing the other women take up — she'd rather not, and dares him to call her unladylike. He just shrugs that everyone has their talents, admits he can't cook either, and pitches in, earning a gruff nod of approval.
ranks.B: Catching Sully hauling heavy gear around camp, Chrom learns it's deliberate strength training, and the two spar to test it. He comes away genuinely impressed by the force behind her blows while she rates his defense near-impenetrable, and they fall to reminiscing about wrestling with sticks and climbing trees as children.
ranks.A: Chrom tells Sully he hopes she never changes — she's the only woman he can still spar with and be wholly himself around — and she finally admits the insecurity under the bravado about not being a proper lady. He insists she's perfect as she is and calls her the anchor that steadies him; emboldened, she asks outright whether he might see her as more than a friend.
ranks.S: Picking up the anchor between them, Chrom pledges his support for every day to come and gives Sully the royal signet ring made at his birth. Touched past her usual bluntness, she accepts and admits she loves him — and probably always has.

ranks.C: Maribelle fusses over Chrom with tea and devotion, insisting it's a noblewoman's honor to serve royalty. He gently pushes back that in war a king, a noble, and a peasant are all just comrades-in-arms, and asks her — for his sake — not to wear herself out looking after him.
ranks.B: After Maribelle takes a reckless risk to shield him, Chrom tells her plainly that they must fight as equals or not win at all, and that losing her would be unbearable. She arrives with tea and crumpets spread with gooseberry jam, his boyhood favorite — a detail Lissa let slip — and confesses how she envies the lifelong closeness he shares with his sister.
ranks.A: Maribelle frets that Chrom is forever surrounded by elegant court ladies who make her feel plain, then blurts out aloud her worry that he'll never find room in his heart for her. Mortified the instant it escapes her, she begs him to pretend he didn't hear and blames the whole thing on battle fatigue.
ranks.S: Chrom laughs off the glittering court she imagines — calling its duties a loathsome bore — and makes clear she's the one he wants. He proposes with the royal ring of Ylisse, and the sharp-tongued noblewoman accepts with uncharacteristic joy.

ranks.C: Robin asks why Chrom took in an amnesiac stranger found face-down in a field, and is unsettled when the answer is simply that she needed help — that's who he is. Grateful but alarmed by his recklessness, she lectures him to be more careful; he cheerfully refuses to change.
ranks.B: Pressed on why he wanders into danger himself instead of sending soldiers, Chrom answers: because of her — had he not gone out in person, they'd never have met. To him those bonds are the true strength of the army, and Robin, won over, insists on coming along to watch his back and help him forge more.
ranks.A: An offhand remark from Chrom that he'd never quite thought of Robin as a lady lands badly, and he spends the rest of the conversation digging himself deeper with increasingly tangled apologies. The comedy of it only underlines how comfortable and close the two have grown.
ranks.S: Cornered by Robin for avoiding her, Chrom finally forces the words out — ally, then comrade, then friend, then something more — steadying himself with deep breaths before admitting he cares for her as a man does a woman. He proposes, and the partnership that carried the army becomes a vow to face whatever comes side by side.
